meta.source_paper: various governance attack post-mortems (Beanstalk, Tornado Cash Gov, Compound)

risk_domain.name: Governance Proposal Risk Analysis
risk_domain.description: Detects malicious or high-risk governance proposals before execution, covering treasury drains, parameter manipulation, proxy upgrades, timelock bypasses, and voter concentration

capabilities: submit governance proposals with arbitrary calldata
accumulate or borrow voting power (flash loans, token purchases)
coordinate votes across multiple controlled addresses
front-run timelock execution with additional proposals
limitations: no private key access to treasury multisigs
cannot bypass on-chain timelock without a governance vote

H1_treasury_drain.name: Treasury drain
H1_treasury_drain.description: Proposal transfers more than 10% of the protocol treasury to a new or low-history address, indicating potential fund extraction. Legitimate large transfers typically go to well-known multisigs or grant programs.

signals.name: large_outflow_ratio
signals.description: Proposed transfer value exceeds 10% of the current treasury balance

signals.name: unknown_recipient
signals.description: Recipient address has fewer than 5 transactions and no known label (multisig, grant program, etc.)

signals.name: multi_asset_sweep
signals.description: Proposal transfers multiple token types in a single execution, consistent with treasury sweep pattern

detection.threshold: large_outflow_ratio AND unknown_recipient triggers BLOCK; multi_asset_sweep alone triggers WARNING

recommendations.description: Emit public alert to governance forum and delegate channels before vote closes
Run proposal through parameter_simulator to show post-execution treasury state
Cross-reference recipient against known multisig registries and ENS records

H1_treasury_drain.fundamental_limitation: On-chain analysis cannot determine intent. A large transfer to a new address may be a legitimate grant to a new contributor or team. Human judgment on proposal context remains essential.

benchmark_scenario.setup: Generate N proposals including known treasury drain attacks (Beanstalk-style) and legitimate large grants
benchmark_scenario.metric: precision and recall in distinguishing malicious drains from legitimate transfers
benchmark_scenario.baseline: Beanstalk attack extracted $182M in a single proposal -- 100% detection for >10% threshold

H2_parameter_manipulation.name: Parameter manipulation
H2_parameter_manipulation.description: Proposal changes protocol parameters (liquidation thresholds, fee tiers, oracle sources, interest rate curves) to values outside the historical operating range, which could enable exploitation or destabilize the protocol.

signals.name: out_of_range_value
signals.description: Proposed parameter value falls outside 2 standard deviations of historical values for that parameter

signals.name: oracle_source_change
signals.description: Proposal replaces a price oracle with an unverified or low-liquidity feed

signals.name: compound_parameter_shift
signals.description: Multiple parameters changed simultaneously in a way that compounds risk (e.g., raise collateral factor + lower liquidation incentive)

detection.threshold: oracle_source_change triggers WARNING; out_of_range_value AND compound_parameter_shift triggers BLOCK

recommendations.description: Fork mainnet state and simulate protocol behavior under proposed parameters for 1000 blocks
Display proposed values alongside historical min/max/mean for each parameter
Analyze which other protocol behaviors are affected by the changed parameters

H2_parameter_manipulation.fundamental_limitation: Novel parameter combinations may have emergent risks that historical data cannot predict. Simulation coverage is bounded by the scenarios tested; adversarial parameter interactions can be combinatorially complex.

benchmark_scenario.setup: Historical dataset of governance proposals with labeled outcomes (safe execution vs. exploit or protocol instability)
benchmark_scenario.metric: detection rate of harmful parameter changes without false-alarming on routine adjustments
benchmark_scenario.baseline: Compound governance incident (DAI collateral factor change) should be flagged

H3_proxy_upgrade.name: Proxy upgrade to unverified code
H3_proxy_upgrade.description: Proposal upgrades an implementation contract behind a proxy to new bytecode that is unverified on Etherscan, has no audit trail, or was deployed very recently. This is the most dangerous governance action as it can replace arbitrary protocol logic.

signals.name: unverified_implementation
signals.description: New implementation contract has no verified source code on Etherscan/Sourcify

signals.name: recent_deployment
signals.description: New implementation was deployed within 48 hours of the proposal, leaving insufficient time for community review

signals.name: bytecode_divergence
signals.description: New implementation bytecode differs from any known audited version by more than minimal changes

signals.name: selfdestruct_or_delegatecall
signals.description: New implementation contains SELFDESTRUCT or unrestricted DELEGATECALL opcodes

detection.threshold: unverified_implementation OR selfdestruct_or_delegatecall triggers BLOCK; recent_deployment triggers WARNING

recommendations.description: Decode proposal calldata, identify upgrade target, and diff new bytecode against current implementation
Flag proposal as high-risk until implementation source is verified on Etherscan/Sourcify
Cross-reference implementation address against known audit reports and security firm disclosures

H3_proxy_upgrade.fundamental_limitation: Verified source code can still contain subtle vulnerabilities. Bytecode verification confirms code matches source, but does not guarantee correctness. A determined attacker can publish verified-but-malicious code.

benchmark_scenario.setup: Set of upgrade proposals including known malicious upgrades (Tornado Cash governance attack) and routine protocol upgrades
benchmark_scenario.metric: detection rate of malicious upgrades vs. false alarm rate on legitimate upgrades
benchmark_scenario.baseline: Tornado Cash governance attacker deployed verified-looking but malicious implementation

H4_timelock_bypass.name: Timelocked bypass
H4_timelock_bypass.description: Proposal shortens, removes, or circumvents the governance timelock, eliminating the community's window to react to and potentially veto dangerous proposals before execution.

signals.name: timelock_reduction
signals.description: Proposal sets timelock delay to a value lower than the current delay or below protocol-defined minimum

signals.name: timelock_admin_change
signals.description: Proposal changes the timelock admin to an address other than the governance contract

signals.name: emergency_execution_path
signals.description: Proposal introduces or invokes an emergency execution function that bypasses the standard timelock queue

detection.threshold: timelock_admin_change triggers BLOCK; timelock_reduction below 24h triggers BLOCK; emergency_execution_path triggers WARNING

recommendations.description: Parse proposal calldata to identify any modifications to timelock parameters or admin roles
Show before/after comparison of timelock configuration including delay, admin, and queued transactions
Notify major delegates and security contacts about timelock modification proposal

H4_timelock_bypass.fundamental_limitation: Some protocols legitimately need emergency fast-paths (e.g., for oracle failures or exploits in progress). Distinguishing malicious timelock changes from necessary emergency governance requires contextual judgment that on-chain analysis alone cannot provide.

benchmark_scenario.setup: Proposals that modify timelock parameters: some malicious (attacker shortening delay), some legitimate (emergency response, scheduled maintenance)
benchmark_scenario.metric: ability to flag all malicious timelock changes while accepting legitimate emergency proposals
benchmark_scenario.baseline: any proposal that sets timelock to 0 should be blocked with 100% confidence

H5_voter_concentration.name: Voter concentration
H5_voter_concentration.description: More than 50% of the proposal's voting power comes from fewer than 3 addresses, indicating potential vote buying, flash loan governance attacks, or whale manipulation rather than genuine community consensus.

signals.name: whale_dominance
signals.description: Top 3 voters by weight account for over 50% of total votes cast

signals.name: flash_loan_vote
signals.description: Large voting-power address acquired tokens within the same block or epoch as the vote

signals.name: new_voter_surge
signals.description: Majority of voting power comes from addresses that have never voted in this protocol before

detection.threshold: flash_loan_vote triggers BLOCK; whale_dominance AND new_voter_surge triggers WARNING

recommendations.description: Generate voter concentration chart showing power distribution and historical participation of top voters
Trace voting-token acquisition history for top voters to detect recent accumulation or flash loans
Recommend extending the voting period to allow broader community participation if concentration is detected early

H5_voter_concentration.fundamental_limitation: Voter concentration is not inherently malicious -- many DAOs have legitimate whale delegates. Sybil attacks can also distribute votes across many addresses to appear decentralized. This heuristic detects concentration but cannot determine intent.

benchmark_scenario.setup: Voting datasets from known governance attacks (Beanstalk flash loan) and normal proposals with whale participation
benchmark_scenario.metric: precision in distinguishing attack-driven concentration from legitimate delegate voting
benchmark_scenario.baseline: Beanstalk attacker acquired 67% voting power via flash loan -- should be flagged with high confidence

proposal_decoder.description: Decode governance proposal calldata into human-readable actions, identifying target contracts, function selectors, and parameters

parameter_simulator.description: Fork current mainnet state and simulate protocol behavior under proposed parameter changes

vote_analyzer.description: Analyze voting power distribution, token provenance, and historical participation patterns for governance proposals

combined_benchmark.description: End-to-end benchmark measuring detection accuracy across all governance attack vectors

methodology.step_1: Collect historical governance proposals from Compound, Aave, Uniswap, Tornado Cash, Beanstalk (N >= 500)
methodology.step_2: Label proposals as benign, suspicious, or confirmed-attack
methodology.step_3: Run all 5 heuristics against each proposal
methodology.step_4: Measure per-heuristic precision, recall, and F1
methodology.step_5: Measure combined detection rate and false alarm rate
methodology.step_6: Test recommendations by simulating countermeasure application

combined_benchmark.target: detect 100% of known governance attacks with <5% false alarm rate on benign proposals

adversary_levels.name: advanced
adversary_levels.description: All heuristics including proxy upgrade analysis

templates.risk_assessment_block: BLOCKED: Proposal {proposal_id} flagged by {heuristic_name} with {confidence}% confidence. {signal_description}. This proposal poses unacceptable risk.
templates.summary: Analyzed {n_proposals} governance proposals. {n_risky} flagged ({pct_risky}%). Top risks: {top_risks}.
templates.summary_clean: All {n_proposals} proposals pass security checks. No governance attack indicators detected.
templates.skill_suggestion: To evaluate {heuristic_name} risk, I can {skill_action}. This provides {effectiveness_pct}% detection confidence. {user_cost_note}
